auth_prerequisites: Set a supported provider API key in the environment or the gptme config directory, or let the first interactive run prompt and save it.
For no-API-key operation, run a local model backend such as Ollama and point gptme at it with `OPENAI_BASE_URL` plus a local model id.
Windows users need WSL or Docker rather than a direct native install.

non_interactive_execution.summary: Official docs show one-shot prompt execution, file arguments, shell pipelines, and chained tasks, so gptme clearly supports non-interactive terminal execution.

offline_strategy.summary: Official docs explicitly document local-model usage without an API key, and the homepage positions gptme as local-first with support for fully local runtimes.

observable_cli_surface.summary: The public docs show a text-first CLI surface with explicit one-shot invocations, stdin pipelines, and tool-rich terminal operation that a wrapper can observe directly.

redaction_fit.summary: gptme documents a separate `config.local.toml` for secrets, environment fallbacks, and a tool allowlist, and the open-source repo gives a reasonable audit surface for wrapper-side redaction policy.

crate_first_fit.summary: gptme has a stable public CLI install surface, a Python package, and explicit terminal workflows, which makes it plausible for a crate-first wrapper even though it is not distributed as a Rust binary upstream.

reproducibility.summary: The retrieved official docs and PyPI listing provide stable public install channels and published Python requirements, but the retrieved getting-started page does not itself define a version-pinning workflow.

future_leverage.summary: gptme appears to have broader leverage than a simple wrapper because the official materials describe plugins, skills, lessons, MCP/ACP, autonomous agents, and an active release cadence.

normalized_caveats: Native Windows is not directly supported; official guidance points users to WSL or Docker.
Some optional tools need extra system dependencies such as Playwright, tmux, or GitHub CLI.
Interactive first run will prompt for provider credentials unless a supported local model backend is preconfigured.

evidence.title: gptme - agent in your terminal
evidence.excerpt: The homepage says gptme runs anywhere a terminal runs, including headless servers and CI pipelines, and is provider-agnostic, local-first, and fully local via `llama.cpp`.

evidence.title: Getting Started — gptme
evidence.excerpt: Getting started recommends `pipx` or `uv`, shows one-shot prompts and shell pipelines, notes WSL/Docker for Windows, and documents local-model use with Ollama and no API key.

evidence.title: Configuration — gptme
evidence.excerpt: Config docs place settings in `~/.config/gptme/config.toml`, allow `config.local.toml` for secrets, and support env fallbacks plus a tool allowlist.

evidence.title: GitHub - gptme/gptme
evidence.excerpt: GitHub describes gptme as free and open source, local-first, active, and extensible with plugins, skills, lessons, MCP/ACP, and autonomous-agent workflows.

evidence.title: gptme · PyPI
evidence.excerpt: PyPI shows `gptme 0.31.0`, Python `>=3.10`, and a terminal-first agent with shell, code execution, file editing, web, vision, and computer-use features.
